variation.emphasis: Stress the procedural and threshold defects first.

transcript.advocate_opening:
Good morning, Your Honor.

This motion should be resolved at the pleading threshold, because the Complaint never gets past the basic federal rules that govern what a plaintiff must allege before imposing discovery burdens on defendants.

At the outset, this is not a case about whether Plaintiff might someday be able to prove some grievance. It is about whether the operative Complaint, as actually pleaded, states a plausible claim for relief under Rules 8, 9(b), and 12(b)(6). It does not.

First, the contract claim fails on elemental pleading grounds. Under Twombly and Iqbal, a plaintiff must do more than recite the labels of a cause of action and assert that it was harmed. Here, Plaintiff never identifies the contractual provision allegedly breached. That omission is fundamental. Without the provision, neither the Court nor Defendants can tell what duty supposedly existed, what conduct supposedly violated that duty, or whether the alleged breach is anything more than speculation. The Complaint also does not plead facts showing satisfaction of any condition precedent. That is another threshold defect, because performance or occurrence of conditions precedent is part of stating a viable contract claim. And on damages, the pleading offers only abstract assertions of harm, not factual allegations making damages plausible. So even if every well-pleaded fact is accepted as true, there is still no plausible breach-of-contract claim as a matter of Rule 8.

Second, the fraud claim fails for the independent and heightened reason that Rule 9(b) demands particularity. The Complaint does not say who made the allegedly false statements, when they were made, where they were made, or how they were communicated to Plaintiff. Those are not technical omissions; they are the core particulars Rule 9(b) requires. The pleading also tries to satisfy scienter by simply characterizing the conduct as “knowing” and “intentional.” That is precisely the sort of conclusory allegation Iqbal rejects and Rule 9(b) does not tolerate. And the problem is compounded by group pleading: alleging that “Defendants” collectively committed fraud does not give any individual defendant notice of the specific misconduct attributed to that defendant, which is exactly why courts reject collective fraud allegations under Rule 9(b).

Third, because these defects are substantive, dismissal should not be delayed by another round of conclusory pleading. Plaintiff has already amended once, and the amended pleading still fails to identify the contract term allegedly breached and still does not plead the basic who, what, when, where, and how of the alleged fraud. At that point, the Court is not dealing with an inartful draft; it is dealing with the absence of facts necessary to state these claims. The proper course is dismissal of the Complaint in its entirety, and dismissal of the fraud claim with prejudice.

So the motion presents a straightforward threshold question: has Plaintiff pleaded facts, as opposed to conclusions, sufficient to state a plausible contract claim and a particularized fraud claim? On this Complaint, the answer to both questions is no.

ARGUMENTS: Contract claim lacks essential facts; Fraud fails Rule 9(b) particularity; Prior amendment shows futility/prejudice dismissal
transcript.opponent_attack:
Defendants’ motion overreaches at the threshold.

First, it is defective as a Rule 12(b)(6) attack because it asks the Court to impose a level of factual granularity the Rules do not require. A complaint need not quote contract language verbatim, attach the agreement, plead evidence of performance, or itemize damages with accountant-level precision. Rule 8 asks for a plausible claim and fair notice, not a trial brief. And Rule 9(c) expressly allows conditions precedent to be pleaded generally. So Defendants’ centerpiece argument—that Plaintiff “never identifies the specific contractual provision breached” and “pleads no facts showing that any condition precedent was satisfied”—fails as a matter of pleading standard unless the Complaint truly says nothing beyond labels. Their own characterization reveals they are demanding particulars beyond the Rule.

Second, the motion is structurally weak because it treats supposed lack of detail as equivalent to absence of an element. Those are not the same. If the Complaint identifies the contract, the parties, the obligations undertaken, Plaintiff’s performance or excuse, Defendants’ concrete nonperformance, and resulting loss, that states a contract claim. Whether Plaintiff can prove the exact clause, the full course of performance, or the measure of damages is a merits question for discovery and summary judgment, not a basis to strike the claim now.

Third, Defendants misstate the fraud pleading burden. Rule 9(b) requires particularity as to the circumstances of fraud, but knowledge and intent may be alleged generally. Their complaint that scienter is pleaded by alleging “knowing” and “intentional” conduct is therefore not a defect by itself. Nor does Rule 9(b) demand impossible precision before discovery where key internal facts are in Defendants’ control. The question is whether the Complaint identifies the who, what, when, where, and how with enough specificity to give notice and protect against a fishing expedition—not whether it pleads every evidentiary detail Defendants would prefer.

On the contract claim specifically, Defendants’ attack is vulnerable on each alleged omission:

- **Specific provision breached.** There is no federal rule requiring a plaintiff to cite section-and-verse of the contract. Identifying the agreement, its subject matter, the operative promise, and the way Defendants failed to perform is enough at this stage. Motions to dismiss are denied routinely where the complaint alleges the contractual duty in substance rather than by quotation.
- **Conditions precedent.** Rule 9(c) squarely defeats this argument. A general allegation that Plaintiff performed all conditions precedent or that they occurred is sufficient. Defendants cannot convert a rule permitting general pleading into a dismissal standard requiring detailed proof.
- **Damages.** Pleading “damages only in the abstract” is not fatal where the Complaint alleges a concrete business loss, out-of-pocket injury, lost payment, diminished value, or other nonperformance-linked harm. Rule 8 does not require a damages spreadsheet. If Defendants want more specificity, that is discovery territory.

The fraud arguments are equally overstated.

- **Who/when/where/how.** If the Complaint identifies the transaction, the substance of the false statements, the time period, the context in which they were made, why they were false, and Plaintiff’s reliance, that ordinarily satisfies Rule 9(b). Defendants act as though only exact dates, exact quotations, and exact mediums count. That is not the law.
- **Group pleading.** Collective allegations are improper only when they obscure each defendant’s role. But where the Complaint distinguishes the roles played by the participants in a unified scheme, Rule 9(b) is satisfied. Defendants’ motion simply assumes impermissible group pleading; it does not establish that the Complaint fails to tie misrepresentations or conduct to specific actors.
- **Scienter.** As noted, Rule 9(b) expressly permits malice, intent, knowledge, and other conditions of mind to be alleged generally. Defendants are trying to erase that sentence from the Rule.

Most importantly, even if the Court thinks any aspect of either count could be sharpened, dismissal **with prejudice** is unjustifiable. That request is the weakest part of the motion. Pleading deficiencies are classically curable by amendment. The fact that Plaintiff has amended once does not remotely establish futility absent a prior judicial ruling identifying deficiencies and a failure to cure, or some legal bar that no amendment could overcome. Defendants offer neither. Their insistence that the defects are “substantive rather than form” is just argument; they do not show futility as a matter of law. At most, they describe matters of detail. That is the paradigm case for denying dismissal or, if the Court wants more specificity, allowing leave to amend.

So the Court has at least three clean grounds to deny this motion:

1. Defendants apply the wrong pleading standard by demanding evidentiary detail rather than plausibility and notice.
2. Rule 9(c) independently forecloses the conditions-precedent argument.
3. Rule 9(b) does not require the hyper-technical specificity Defendants demand, and in any event does not support dismissal with prejudice on this record.

In short, the motion’s real defect is that it confuses “not as detailed as Defendants want” with “not legally sufficient.” That is not Rule 12(b)(6), and it is not Rule 9(b).

ATTACK: Wrong-Standard Overreach
transcript.bench_pressure_points: Counsel, your motion speaks at a high level about Twombly and Iqbal, but I need a claim-by-claim, element-by-element showing tied to what is actually alleged in the complaint, not just the assertion that the pleading is “conclusory.” On the contract claim, why isn’t Rule 9(c) fatal to your conditions-precedent argument, and what authority says a plaintiff must identify the precise contract provision at the pleading stage rather than plead the contractual obligation and the alleged nonperformance with enough clarity to give notice? And on fraud, if you want dismissal with prejudice because plaintiff already amended once, I will want to know whether the prior amendment had the benefit of your present Rule 9(b) objections and why the defects are truly substantive rather than curable by a more definite pleading.
transcript.advocate_rebuttal:
Defendants are not demanding evidentiary detail or verbatim contract quotations; the point is narrower and dispositive: the Complaint, as pleaded, does not identify any concrete contractual duty that was breached, does not even generally allege under Rule 9(c) that conditions precedent were satisfied or excused, and alleges damages only as abstract harm, so it never moves from legal elements to a plausible breach claim. Rule 9(c) permits general pleading of conditions precedent, but it does not permit silence, and Twombly/Iqbal still require enough factual content to tell the Court what contractual obligation was owed, how it was not performed, and what loss followed.

The fraud claim fails for the same reason at Rule 9(b)’s threshold: this is not a quarrel about missing minutiae, but about the absence of the basic who, what, when, where, how, and any nonconclusory basis for scienter, compounded by impermissible group pleading against all Defendants. And dismissal with prejudice is warranted because Plaintiff has already amended once yet still has not supplied those foundational fraud particulars, confirming that the defect is substantive—failure to plead an actual fraudulent misrepresentation with particularity—not merely a matter of rephrasing.
transcript.bench_final:
The motion is granted. The complaint, as described in the briefing, does not cross the pleading threshold because it fails to identify a concrete contractual obligation that was breached and, as to fraud, fails to plead the alleged misrepresentation with Rule 9(b) particularity; absent more, I would dismiss the contract claim without prejudice and the fraud claim with prejudice if plaintiff has already had a fair opportunity to amend.

DECISIVE: the complaint never pleads the specific duty or misrepresentation that makes either claim plausible.
